votes.bill: TikTok Divestment Act (Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act)
votes.note: Required ByteDance to divest TikTok or face a US ban; passed 352-65.
$26.4B in military aid and missile-defense funding for Israel. Aligns with pro-Israel (AIPAC) donors.
~$61B in military and economic aid for Ukraine.
~$8B for Taiwan and Indo-Pacific allies to counter China.
$1.2 trillion appropriations package; averted a shutdown.

votes.bill: Reforming Intelligence and Securing America Act (FISA Section 702 reauthorization)
votes.note: Reauthorized FISA Section 702 surveillance authority for two years.
Directed the Dept. of Education to adopt the IHRA definition of antisemitism in Title VI enforcement.

stockTrades.conflictNote: Exercised 500 call options (acquired Nov 2023) to acquire 50,000 shares while Congress considered chip/AI and semiconductor policy.

stockTrades.conflictNote: Sold 10,000 NVIDIA shares shortly after exercising options.

House Stock Watcher / House Clerk PTR

lawsuits.title: McCarthy v. Pelosi (House proxy-voting challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed; court held the Speech or Debate Clause barred the suit. Sued as Speaker, not personal liability.
lawsuits.description: GOP Leader Kevin McCarthy and members challenged the House rule allowing proxy voting during COVID-19. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity as Speaker.

lawsuits.title: Massie v. Pelosi (proxy voting / floor fines challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed; affirmed by the D.C. Circuit on June 30, 2023 (No. 22-5058). Pelosi sued in her official capacity only.
lawsuits.description: Rep. Thomas Massie and others challenged House proxy voting and floor mask/metal-detector fines. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity as Speaker.

lawsuits.title: Budowich v. Pelosi (Jan. 6 Committee subpoena challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed. Challenge to a House Jan. 6 Select Committee subpoena; Pelosi named as Speaker.
lawsuits.description: Taylor Budowich sued to block a Select Committee subpoena for bank records. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity.

lawsuits.title: Meadows v. Pelosi (Jan. 6 Committee subpoena challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed. Challenge to a House Jan. 6 Select Committee subpoena; Pelosi named as Speaker.
lawsuits.description: Former WH Chief of Staff Mark Meadows sued to block Select Committee subpoenas. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity.

lawsuits.title: Republican National Committee v. Pelosi (Jan. 6 subpoena challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed. Challenge to a Select Committee subpoena of vendor records; Pelosi named as Speaker.
lawsuits.description: The RNC sued to block a Jan. 6 Select Committee subpoena to a third-party vendor. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity.

votes.bill: TikTok Divestment Act (Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act)
votes.note: Required ByteDance to divest TikTok or face a US ban; passed 352-65 with broad bipartisan support.
$26.4B in military aid and missile-defense funding for Israel. Aligns with pro-Israel (AIPAC) donors.
~$61B in military and economic aid for Ukraine.
~$8B for Taiwan and Indo-Pacific allies to counter China.
$1.2 trillion appropriations package funding major federal agencies; averted a shutdown.

votes.bill: Reforming Intelligence and Securing America Act (FISA Section 702 reauthorization)
votes.note: Reauthorized FISA Section 702 warrantless surveillance authority for two years.
Directed the Dept. of Education to adopt the IHRA definition of antisemitism in Title VI enforcement.

viewSummary: Nancy Pelosi raised about $10.0M in her most recent complete election cycle (2023-2024), overwhelmingly from large individual donors. About $9.27M came from large individual donors and $253,300 from PACs, with only roughly $480K from small grassroots donors - a big-money share near 95%. Her largest PAC/industry backers were labor unions (~$99K), finance/insurance/real estate (~$34K), ideological and single-issue groups, agribusiness and pro-Israel (AIPAC) PACs (~$16K). Of 7 tracked House votes, her YEA on the Israel Security Supplemental aligned with the position of pro-Israel (AIPAC) donors; her other tracked votes (TikTok divestment, Ukraine and Indo-Pacific aid, FISA Section 702 reauthorization, the $1.2T appropriations package and the Antisemitism Awareness Act) did not track a specific donor position. Her household's disclosed 2024 stock activity includes large NVIDIA, Apple, Palo Alto Networks and Broadcom positions (about $25M in tracked transactions, several via call options), all flagged as potential conflicts given Congress's role in tech, chip and cybersecurity policy. Legal: 5 federal suits named her as a defendant in her official capacity as Speaker (House proxy-voting challenges by McCarthy and Massie; three Jan. 6 Committee subpoena challenges) - all dismissed, none involving personal liability or wrongdoing.

name: Donald J. Trump
title: President of the United States

lawsuits.title: People of the State of New York v. Trump (NY "hush money" criminal case)
lawsuits.outcome: Convicted May 2024 on 34 felony counts; sentenced Jan 2025 to unconditional discharge.
lawsuits.description: First U.S. president convicted of a felony; payments related to 2016 election.

lawsuits.title: Carroll v. Trump II (defamation & sexual abuse)
lawsuits.outcome: Jury found Trump liable; $5 million in damages.
lawsuits.description: Federal jury found Trump sexually abused and defamed writer E. Jean Carroll.

lawsuits.title: Carroll v. Trump I (defamation)
lawsuits.outcome: Jury awarded $83.3 million; affirmed by Second Circuit.
lawsuits.description: Damages for statements denying Carroll's allegations.

lawsuits.title: People of the State of New York v. Trump (civil fraud)
lawsuits.outcome: ~$454M penalty later vacated by appellate court as excessive (8th Amendment); fraud liability upheld.
lawsuits.description: NY AG action over inflated asset valuations; monetary judgment thrown out on appeal.

lawsuits.title: United States v. Trump (classified documents)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed July 15, 2024 (Judge Cannon ruled special counsel appointment unlawful).
lawsuits.description: Charges over retention of classified documents at Mar-a-Lago.

lawsuits.title: United States v. Trump (2020 election interference) & Georgia RICO case
lawsuits.outcome: Federal case wound down after 2024 election; Georgia case largely stalled.
lawsuits.description: Charges related to efforts to overturn the 2020 election results.

sources: FEC committee totals (Donald J. Trump for President 2024, Inc.)
funding.note: Breakdown is an estimate; itemized vs. small-dollar split for the principal committee is approximate. JFC/super-PAC money (combined ~$1.45B) sits outside the candidate committee.

viewSummary: Donald J. Trump's 2024 principal campaign committee reported about $496M in receipts; counting joint fundraising committees and aligned super PACs, the broader operation raised roughly $1.45 billion (the itemized vs. small-dollar split shown is an estimate). As an executive-branch official he casts no congressional roll-call votes and files no congressional stock disclosures, so those categories are not applicable. His legal record is extensive: a 2024 New York criminal conviction on 34 felony counts, two E. Jean Carroll civil judgments ($5M and $83.3M), a New York civil fraud case (penalty later vacated on appeal, liability upheld), and federal/state cases that were dismissed or wound down.
